text: The laparoscopic camera enters through the umbilical port, revealing the gallbladder and a smooth liver surface. The stomach appears slightly distended with gas. The colon is visible with the tinea coli, and some adhesions are noted. The pelvis is empty, lacking internal genital organs. The surgeon prepares to establish additional ports, identifying the falciform ligament along the liver's border. A skin incision is made, and a cannula is inserted using a screwing motion into the peritoneal cavity. The trocar is removed, and the next port is placed on the right side in the anterior axillary line.

A 5mm port is established for the fundal grasper, followed by the placement of an additional port for the infinitum holder. The surgeon begins to shear down mental attachments, pulling them down to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon gently removes inflammatory attachments from the neck of the gallbladder, revealing the common bile duct (CBD) and duodenum. The dissection focuses on Calot's triangle, carefully separating thickened omentum and identifying the cystic artery and its posterior branch. Fibrous threads are pulled off from the peritoneum, resembling fibers of a fabric.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and achieves the critical view of safety by dissecting up to the lower lung of the gallbladder fossa. Two windows are created by clearing small fascia and fibrous tissue from both sides, ensuring a clear view for safe clipping and cutting.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct, with a visible lymph node nearby, preparing to clamp these structures.

The surgeon prepares to cut the cystic duct after securing it with clips. The gallbladder is mobilized from the summit of the fundus, checking for any bleeding points and cauterizing as necessary. The gallbladder is then removed from the fossa.
